author: marioevz
content: Thanks, we did not implement the negative case (contract not being there) in eels because afaik it requires knowing in the transition tool interface whether the block being processed is the fork activation block or not, and this functionality is not implemented. Does this affect the witness only and not the BAL?

author: jsign
content:
Did some research about this in the Geth codebase.

This [ApplyEIP7997(..)](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/blob/6e6fcef0ba6cbf3768706b42845cec5a9e99499a/consensus/misc/eip7997.go#L30) gets executed before Geth activates "BAL tracking" -- so for them, this extra lookup is not captured in BALs. Which I imagine makes sense with all the current testing status of Geth reg Amsterdam. Not sure if this was intentional or not; but I guess makes sense.

For execution witness, this is lookup appears as an extra MPT nodes added to `executionWitness.state` yes.  Looks like Geth has two separate mechanism to add stuff into BALs and executionWitness -- not sure I would recommend this in general for EL implemetnations since in EEST we intentionally use the same state tracker for both BAL and executionWitness to be coherent.

I actually ran a fixture from EIP-8025 for a transition fork, and indeed see Geth adding this extra lookup there.

So TL;DR for Geth:
1. Yes, they do this lookup to see if they have to deploy the factory contract automatically (this is not exactly what EEST Amsterdam spec define IIUC).
2. This state lookup is not detected for BALs, since "BAL tracking activation" happens after this `ApplyEIP3997(...)` call.
3. This state lookup it is detected and results in a different `executionWitness.state` extra item(s) addition, since Geth uses their prefetcher for executionWitness construction -- so it doesn't depend on the "BAL mechanism".

The good part is that this is detected by the executionWitness EEST test runs in BPO2ToAmsterdamAt15k cases... I guess eventually we would need to figure out how this situation should be handeled.
IIUC, technically, if EEST does not define this lookup, then that's the source of truth and should be "removed" somehow from Geth detection or similar.

author: jsign
content: I imagine for Nimbus might happen something similar, although <@510413979080720385> might confirm on his side.

author: deme1744
content:
Yes, so in Nimbus we have the same behaviour as Geth basically. BALs exclude it, the witness tracking does not (our tracking is currently also separated like geth)
I have a PR ready with a fix so that witness tracking is also not yet enabled on that EIP7997 related access. But I'm awaiting with merging that until its agreed that we omit it from the witness.

author: jsign
content: These situations where things are "too open" to EL implementations feels quite dangerous to me

author: jsign
content: Looks like ELs not always follows exactly what EEST spec define?

author: deme1744
content:
Side note: As mentioned we also do the witness tracking and bal tracking separately, and yes I have also already realized that it would keep us from shooting us in the foot over and over again if we were to get that together. It is however historically like this, and has to do with witness tracking being there before BAL already (and the old witness format with keys), etc.

I do want to eventually get that better, but I first want to focus just getting our current implementation fully working with all test cases.
